title: Challenging Case and Mineral Disease
authors: Neil J. L. Gittoes, BSc, PhD, FRCP

items.title: Why challenging bone and mineral cases need individualized attention
items.text: Rarer diseases and rare events in common conditions require considered individualized approaches beyond formulaic fracture-risk protocols.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Assessment and management of rarer bone and mineral diseases, or rare events in common conditions, require a considered and individualized approach."

items.title: How hypocalcemia can mimic primary psychiatric disease
items.text: Neuropsychiatric features including mood disturbance are recognized in hypocalcemia; papilledema is a rare association—classic Chvostek/Trousseau signs may be absent despite profound hypocalcemia.
items.reference: Case 1: "Neuropsychiatric presentations, including mood disturbance, are recognized features of hypocalcemia. There is also a rare but recognized association between hypocalcemia and papilledema."

items.title: Why bisphosphonates can cause subtrochanteric fractures in osteogenesis imperfecta
items.text: Even in high fracture-risk OI, bisphosphonate suppression of turnover is linked to bilateral incomplete subtrochanteric fractures—requiring close sequential risk/benefit reassessment.
items.reference: Case 2: "Paradoxically, in this case, such treatment is linked to the evolution of bilateral incomplete subtrochanteric fractures."

items.title: How to manage impending atypical femoral fracture on bisphosphonate
items.text: Withhold further antiresorptives; teriparatide is the most appropriate listed option to promote healing despite unlicensed indication—romosozumab lacks data here.
items.reference: Case 2: "Teriparatide (Answer E), a bone anabolic agent, was commenced to promote fracture healing (no licensed indication)."

items.title: Why TIO can masquerade as neuromuscular disease
items.text: Progressive pain, myopathy, and weakness with elevated ALP and hypophosphatemia may evade diagnosis for years until phosphate is measured and an ethmoid sinus mass is found.
items.reference: Case 3: "This patient had little symptomatic benefit from treatment with phosphate and activated vitamin D. MRI showed a right ethmoid sinus mass"

items.title: How brown tumors differ from primary bone neoplasms
items.text: Brown tumors are rare pseudotumoral hyperparathyroidism manifestations—microscopically similar to giant-cell tumors but diagnosed by biochemical context of severe secondary hyperparathyroidism.
items.reference: Case 4: "Brown tumors are very rare pseudotumoral manifestations of hyperparathyroidism."

items.title: Why celiac disease and pregnancy can unmask severe osteomalacia
items.text: Celiac malabsorption with pregnancy increased demands can produce osteomalacia, secondary hyperparathyroidism, and brown tumors despite prior partial treatment.
items.reference: Case 4: "Celiac disease and pregnancy had contributed to the severe expression of osteomalacia."

items.title: Which factors predict hungry bone syndrome after parathyroidectomy
items.text: Elevated ALP, radiographic parathyroid bone disease, large adenoma, and untreated vitamin D deficiency increase risk—older, not younger, age is associated.
items.reference: Case 5: "Older, rather than younger age, is more often associated with hungry bone syndrome (thus, Answer B is correct)."

items.title: How pregnancy and lactation-associated osteoporosis presents
items.text: Very rare condition with symptomatic postpartum vertebral fractures, no identified cause, and ~20–30% fracture risk in subsequent pregnancies.
items.reference: Case 6: "Fractures in subsequent pregnancies occur in approximately 20% to 30% of cases, and it is prudent to warn patients about this risk"

items.title: Why bisphosphonates are avoided in childbearing years with PLAO
items.text: Teriparatide may be used for severe progressive vertebral fractures; bisphosphonates are not ideal in women of childbearing age due to potential teratogenicity.
items.reference: Case 6: "Bisphosphonates are not ideal in women during their childbearing years due to potential teratogenicity (thus, Answer C is correct)"

items.title: How a limited investigation panel diagnoses most mineral disorders
items.text: With clear understanding of calcium, phosphate, ALP, PTH, 25-OHD, magnesium, and urinary calcium, most conditions can be reliably diagnosed.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Evaluation of bone and mineral metabolism disorders relies on a limited set of investigations; with clear understanding and appropriate interpretation of these, most conditions can be reliably diagnosed and managed."

items.title: Why awareness of rare drug effects prevents harm
items.text: Overlooking bisphosphonate atypical femoral fractures and denosumab rebound vertebral fractures can cause preventable patient harm.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Awareness of thresholds of intervention for treatment and rare adverse effects of commonly used drugs may be overlooked, potentially causing patient harm"

items.question: A 42-year-old man has panic attacks, bilateral visual field constriction, papilledema, and personality change. MRI and LP are normal. Most likely unifying diagnosis?
items.explanation: Labs showed calcium 5.3 mg/dL and PTH 2.0 pg/mL (idiopathic hypoparathyroidism); vision and personality improved with calcium and alfacalcidol.
items.reference: Case 1: "Answer: B) Hypocalcemia"

items.question: In Case 1, Chvostek and Trousseau signs were absent despite calcium 5.3 mg/dL. Best interpretation?
options: Classic signs may be absent despite significant hypocalcemia
Means vitamin D deficiency is the cause
items.explanation: Classic eponymous signs are insensitive—profound hypocalcemia can exist without them.
items.reference: Case 1: "Classic signs of hypocalcemia (eg, Chvostek sign) may be absent despite significant hypocalcemia."

items.question: A 64-year-old woman with OI type 4 on pamidronate then zoledronic acid has bilateral incomplete subtrochanteric femur fractures. Best pharmacologic step?
items.explanation: Further antiresorptives worsen suppressed turnover; teriparatide promotes healing in this setting.
items.reference: Case 2: "Answer: E) Switch to teriparatide"

items.question: Why is continuing zoledronic acid incorrect in Case 2?
options: It has no effect on BMD in OI
Antiresorptives exacerbate suppressed turnover linked to atypical femoral fractures
items.explanation: Persisting with antiresorptives exacerbates turnover suppression causally linked to AFF.
items.reference: Case 2: "Persisting with an antiresorptive agent such as zoledronic acid (Answer A) or denosumab (Answer C) is incorrect, as such treatments exacerbate suppression of bone turnover"

items.question: A 42-year-old man has 3 years of foot/ankle pain, severe myopathy, phosphate 1.6 mg/dL, low 1,25-D, and FGF-23 251 U/L. Most likely diagnosis?
items.explanation: FGF-23 elevation with hypophosphatemia and adult onset point to TIO; ethmoid sinus mass was ultimately identified.
items.reference: Case 3: "Answer: B) Tumor-induced osteomalacia"

items.question: A 21-week pregnant woman with celiac disease has a destructive tibial lesion with giant cells on biopsy. Calcium 7.8 mg/dL, PTH 482 pg/mL, 25-OHD <6 ng/mL. Most likely cause?
items.explanation: Severe osteomalacia with secondary hyperparathyroidism produced brown tumors; biochemistry distinguishes metabolic from primary bone neoplasms.
items.reference: Case 4: "Answer: A) Brown tumor"

items.question: Why is giant-cell tumor less likely than brown tumor in Case 4?
options: Giant-cell tumors never occur in pregnancy
The degree of biochemical abnormality points to metabolic hyperparathyroidism
Brown tumors never occur in celiac disease
PTH is always normal in brown tumors
items.explanation: Marked hypocalcemia, extreme ALP, and very high PTH provide metabolic context favoring brown tumor.
items.reference: Case 4: "a diagnosis of brown tumor of hyperparathyroidism is based on abnormal biochemistry."

items.question: A 25-year-old woman with large parathyroid adenoma, ALP >900 U/L, and orbital brown tumor undergoes parathyroidectomy. Which is NOT a hungry bone risk factor?
items.explanation: Older age—not younger—increases hungry bone risk after parathyroidectomy.
items.reference: Case 5: "Answer: B) Younger age"

items.question: A 28-year-old woman has multiple postpartum vertebral fractures, Z-score -4.3 at spine, and active edema on MRI. Best true statement?
options: Subsequent pregnancy fracture risk is not increased
Bone-active drugs must be continued lifelong
Bisphosphonates should be avoided if possible due to teratogenicity concerns
Fractures typically appear in the second trimester
items.explanation: PLAO fractures occur postpartum; bisphosphonates are avoided in childbearing years when possible; subsequent pregnancy carries 20–30% recurrence risk.
items.reference: Case 6: "Answer: C) Bisphosphonates should be avoided if possible to avoid later potential teratogenicity"

items.question: For severe progressive PLAO vertebral fractures, which therapy was used successfully?
items.explanation: Due to severity and active progression, teriparatide was used with improved Z-score and pain resolution over 2 years.
items.reference: Case 6: "due to the severity and active progression of the vertebral fractures, she is treated with teriparatide."

items.question: A patient has mild hypercalcemia and low urinary calcium. Besides clearance ratio, what now aids FHH vs PHPT distinction?
items.explanation: Genetic panel testing for FHH is increasingly accessible to distinguish from PHPT.
items.reference: Mineral Disease: "with genetic panel testing for FHH being more readily accessible, this approach can also be used to help distinguish the condition from primary hyperparathyroidism."

items.question: A patient's hip T-score is -2.4 but FRAX is low with no prior fracture. Best principle?
options: Contextualize DXA within full fracture-risk profile
items.explanation: T-scores must not be isolated treatment thresholds.
items.reference: Bone Disease: "DXA-determined data should always be contextualized to the full fracture risk profile of the patient."

items.question: A patient stops denosumab without bridging therapy and develops multiple vertebral fractures. What was overlooked?
options: Awareness of denosumab rebound and need for transition therapy
items.explanation: Rebound vertebral fractures after denosumab cessation are a recognized rare adverse effect requiring anticipatory planning.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "rebound vertebral fractures with denosumab."

items.statement: Papilledema can occur as a rare manifestation of hypocalcemia.
items.explanation: A recognized though rare association exists between hypocalcemia and papilledema.
items.reference: Case 1: "There is also a rare but recognized association between hypocalcemia and papilledema."

items.statement: Continuing bisphosphonates is appropriate when incomplete atypical femoral fractures are present.
items.explanation: Further antiresorptive therapy exacerbates turnover suppression linked to AFF.
items.reference: Case 2: "Persisting with an antiresorptive agent such as zoledronic acid (Answer A) or denosumab (Answer C) is incorrect"

items.statement: Brown tumors are histologically indistinguishable from giant-cell tumors.
items.explanation: Microscopically brown tumors resemble giant-cell tumors; diagnosis relies on biochemistry.
items.reference: Case 4: "Microscopically, these lesions are indistinguishable from giant-cell tumors or aneurysmal bone cysts"

items.statement: Younger age is a recognized risk factor for hungry bone syndrome.
items.explanation: Older rather than younger age is more often associated with hungry bone syndrome.
items.reference: Case 5: "Older, rather than younger age, is more often associated with hungry bone syndrome"

items.statement: Pregnancy and lactation-associated osteoporosis fractures typically occur in the postpartum period.
items.explanation: Fractures typically occur postpartum, not during the second trimester.
items.reference: Case 6: "Fractures typically occur in the postpartum period (thus, Answer E is incorrect)"

items.statement: Patients with PLAO should be warned about fracture risk in subsequent pregnancies.
items.explanation: Approximately 20–30% have fractures in subsequent pregnancies.
items.reference: Case 6: "Fractures in subsequent pregnancies occur in approximately 20% to 30% of cases"

items.statement: Tumor-induced osteomalacia may present with progressive weakness mimicking neuromuscular disease.
items.explanation: Years of misdiagnosis occurred before phosphate measurement and tumor localization.
items.reference: Case 3: "The progressive neurogenic or myogenic weakness has affected all 4 limbs to the extent that he stopped working."

items.statement: Calcitonin measurement is routinely indicated in calcium metabolism disorders.
items.explanation: Calcitonin is confined to medullary thyroid carcinoma monitoring.
items.reference: Mineral Disease: "There is no role for calcitonin measurement in the routine investigation of calcium and bone metabolism."

items.assertion: Assertion: Hypocalcemia can present with papilledema and personality change mimicking psychiatric disease.
items.reason: Reason: Neuropsychiatric features are recognized manifestations of hypocalcemia.
items.explanation: Both true and causally linked—profound hypocalcemia explains the neuro-ophthalmic and behavioral presentation.
items.reference: Case 1: "Neuropsychiatric presentations, including mood disturbance, are recognized features of hypocalcemia."

items.assertion: Assertion: Teriparatide is preferred over continuing zoledronic acid for incomplete AFF in OI.
items.reason: Reason: Antiresorptives suppress bone turnover linked to atypical femoral fractures.
items.explanation: Both true—anabolic therapy promotes healing while further antiresorption worsens the causal pathway.
items.reference: Case 2: "such treatments exacerbate suppression of bone turnover, thought to be causally linked to atypical femoral fractures."

items.assertion: Assertion: A destructive bone lesion with giant cells in severe secondary hyperparathyroidism is most likely a brown tumor.
items.reason: Reason: Brown tumors are diagnosed based on abnormal biochemistry rather than histology alone.
items.explanation: Both true—biochemical hyperparathyroidism/osteomalacia context distinguishes brown tumor from primary neoplasm.
items.reference: Case 4: "a diagnosis of brown tumor of hyperparathyroidism is based on abnormal biochemistry."

items.assertion: Assertion: Younger age protects against hungry bone syndrome after parathyroidectomy.
items.reason: Reason: Elevated alkaline phosphatase indicates increased bone remodeling.
items.explanation: Both false—older age is associated with hungry bone; elevated ALP is a risk factor, not a reason younger age protects.
items.reference: Case 5: "Older, rather than younger age, is more often associated with hungry bone syndrome"

items.assertion: Assertion: Bisphosphonates are first-line therapy for PLAO in women of childbearing age.
items.reason: Reason: Bisphosphonates reduce vertebral fracture risk in postmenopausal osteoporosis.
items.explanation: A is false due to teratogenicity concerns; R is true in postmenopausal osteoporosis but irrelevant to PLAO first-line use.
items.reference: Case 6: "Bisphosphonates are not ideal in women during their childbearing years due to potential teratogenicity"

items.assertion: Assertion: Elevated FGF-23 with hypophosphatemia in an adult suggests tumor-induced osteomalacia until proven otherwise.
items.reason: Reason: FGF-23 mediates renal phosphate wasting in TIO.
items.explanation: Both true—adult-onset FGF-23-mediated hypophosphatemia warrants tumor search.
items.reference: Case 3: "Answer: B) Tumor-induced osteomalacia"

items.assertion: Assertion: Stopping denosumab without transition therapy can cause multiple vertebral fractures.
items.reason: Reason: Denosumab has a long skeletal half-life like bisphosphonates.
items.explanation: A is true but R is false—denosumab offset is rapid with rebound, unlike long bisphosphonate binding.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "rebound vertebral fractures with denosumab."

items.assertion: Assertion: Absent Chvostek sign excludes significant hypocalcemia.
items.reason: Reason: Chvostek sign is a classic manifestation of hypocalcemia.
items.explanation: Both false—classic signs may be absent despite profound hypocalcemia.
items.reference: Case 1: "Classic signs of hypocalcemia (eg, Chvostek sign) may be absent despite significant hypocalcemia."
